questions.text: The outer layer of the cerebrum is called:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.explanation: The outer layer of the cerebrum is called the cerebral cortex. The corpus callosum is a large band of axons between the two cerebral hemispheres. The cerebral cortex is involved in the interpretation of sensory information and voluntary action along with intelligence, reasoning, and memory.

questions.text: A typical neuron at rest:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Is more positive outside than inside
options.explanation: A typical neuron is more positive outside than inside due to the presence of Na+ K+ pump which countertransports 3Na+ outside and 2K+ inside, removing one extra positive charge outside every time movement occurs.

options.text: Is more negative outside than inside
options.explanation: At rest neuron is more positive outside

options.text: Has no charge on either side
options.explanation: Counter-transports of ions occurs in an unequal amount resulting in charges

options.text: Has an equal charge on either side
options.explanation: 3 Na+ move outside and 2K+ inside via sodium-potassium pump therefore unequal charge on either side.

questions.explanation: Option [A] A typical neuron is more positive outside than inside due to the presence of Na+ K+ pump which countertransports 3Na+ outside and 2K+ inside, removing one extra positive charge outside every time movement occurs.Option[B] At rest neuron is more positive outsideOption [c] Counter-transports of ions occurs in an unequal amount resulting in chargesOption[D] 3 Na+ move outside and 2K+ inside via sodium-potassium pump therefore unequal charge on either side

questions.text: The nerve impulse which jumps from node to node in myelinated neurons is called as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Resting membrane potential of a cell is defined as the difference in electrical potential across the plasma membrane when the cell is not stimulated or when the cell is in a state of relaxation.

Saltatory conduction describes how an electrical impulse skips from node to node down the full length of an axon, speeding the arrival of the impulse at the nerve terminal compared to the slower continuous progression of depolarization spreading down an unmyelinated axon.

Threshold stimulus in muscle contraction is defined as the minimum strength which is required for the stimuli to instigate the response in a muscle contraction. If the strength of the stimulus is below the threshold stimulus, no response is seen.
As per the explanation, this option is incorrect.

questions.explanation: Saltatory conduction describes how an electrical impulse skips from node to node down the full length of an axon, speeding the arrival of the impulse at the nerve terminal compared to the slower continuous progression of depolarization spreading down an unmyelinated axon.

questions.text: The CNS is protected by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Three layers of membranes known as meninges protect the brain and spinal cord. The delicate inner layer is the pia mater. The middle layer is the arachnoid, a web-like structure filled with fluid that cushions the brain.The tough outer layer is called the dura mater.

This option is incorrect.Three layers of membranes known as meninges protect the brain and spinal cord. The delicate inner layer is the pia mater. The middle layer is the arachnoid, a web-like structure filled with fluid that cushions the brain.The tough outer layer is called the dura mater.

This option is incorrect.Three layers of membranes known as meninges protect the brain and spinal cord. The delicate inner layer is the pia mater. The middle layer is the arachnoid, a web-like structure filled with fluid that cushions the brain.The tough outer layer is called the dura mater.

This option is incorrect.Three layers of membranes known as meninges protect the brain and spinal cord. The delicate inner layer is the pia mater. The middle layer is the arachnoid, a web-like structure filled with fluid that cushions the brain.The tough outer layer is called the dura mater.

questions.explanation: The CNS is covered with three layers of protective coverings called meninges. The outermost layer is the dura mater having its primary function being the protection of the brain and the spinal cord. The dura mater also contains vein-like structures that carry blood from the brain back to the heart. The middle layer is the web-like arachnoid mater. The last layer is the pia mater, which directly contacts and covers the brain and spinal cord like plastic wrap. The space between the arachnoid and pia mater is filled with cerebrospinal fluid

questions.text: White matter of spinal cord is made up of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect.White matter is found in the deeper tissues of the brain (subcortical). It contains nerve fibres (axons), which are extensions of nerve cells (neurons). Many of these nerve fibres are surrounded by a type of sheath or covering called myelin. Myelin gives the white matter its colour.

White matter is found in the deeper tissues of the brain (subcortical). It contains nerve fibres (axons), which are extensions of nerve cells (neurons). Many of these nerve fibres are surrounded by a type of sheath or covering called myelin. Myelin gives the white matter its colour.

This option is incorrect.White matter is found in the deeper tissues of the brain (subcortical). It contains nerve fibres (axons), which are extensions of nerve cells (neurons). Many of these nerve fibres are surrounded by a type of sheath or covering called myelin. Myelin gives the white matter its colour.

This option is incorrect.White matter is found in the deeper tissues of the brain (subcortical). It contains nerve fibres (axons), which are extensions of nerve cells (neurons). Many of these nerve fibres are surrounded by a type of sheath or covering called myelin. Myelin gives the white matter its colour.

questions.explanation: White matter is found in the deeper tissues of the brain (subcortical). It contains nerve fibres (axons), which are extensions of nerve cells (neurons). Many of these nerve fibres are surrounded by a type of sheath or covering called myelin. Myelin gives the white matter its colour.

questions.text: Sense of taste is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Gustation refers to the sense of taste, involving receptors on the tongue detecting chemical substances.
Tactician is not associated with sensory perception. It relates to strategy or planning, not the senses.
Nociception is the perception of pain through specialized nerve endings, unrelated to taste.
Olfaction is the scientific term for the sense of smell, which is different from taste.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Gustation. This term is used to describe the process of detecting taste through taste buds located on the tongue, which respond to chemical substances in food and drink.The option Tactician is unrelated to sensory perception and pertains to strategy or planning. Nociception involves the detection of pain by the nervous system, not related to taste. Olfaction is the term for the sense of smell, a separate sensory system from taste.

questions.text: The number of cortical nephrons are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. The percentage of cortical nephrons is higher than this range.
Correct! Around 80%-90% of nephrons in the human kidney are cortical nephrons. These are located in the outer cortex and have shorter loops of Henle.
This option is incorrect. The percentage of cortical nephrons is higher than this range.
This option is incorrect. The percentage of cortical nephrons exceeds this range.

questions.explanation: Approximately 80%-90% of all nephrons in the human kidney are cortical nephrons. These nephrons are characterized by their glomeruli being located in the outer cortex of the kidney and having relatively short loops of Henle that do not extend deep into the medulla. This makes them the most abundant type of nephrons in the kidney. The other options underestimate the proportion of cortical nephrons in the kidney.

questions.text: Which of the following types of neurons has a single long dendron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: As Sensory neurons have long dendrites to increase their surface area and thus increase their chances of capturing stimuli from the environment. Dendrites are highly branched structures that extend from the cell body of a neuron and receive input from other neurons or from sensory receptors in the environment.

Motor neurons, like sensory neurons, have dendrites that receive signals. However, unlike sensory neurons, the dendrites of motor neurons are typically shorter because their primary function is to transmit signals from the cell body to the axon, which in turn sends signals to the muscles or glands. The dendrites of motor neurons are also less branched than those of sensory neurons.

Associative neurons, also known as interneurons or connector neurons, have short dendrites and axons. Their dendrites receive signals from other neurons, while their axons send signals to other neurons. The short length of their dendrites is due to the fact that they primarily receive input from nearby neurons and do not have to transmit signals over long distances.

Associative neurons, also known as interneurons or connector neurons, have short dendrites and axons. Their dendrites receive signals from other neurons, while their axons send signals to other neurons. The short length of their dendrites is due to the fact that they primarily receive input from nearby neurons and do not have to transmit signals over long distances.

questions.explanation: Sensory neurons have long dendrites to increase their surface area and thus increase their chances of capturing stimuli from the environment. Dendrites are highly branched structures that extend from the cell body of a neuron and receive input from other neurons or from sensory receptors in the environment.

questions.text: In which part of the nervous system will you find Schwann cells?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The central nervous system (CNS) is made up of the brain and spinal cord. It is one of 2 parts of the nervous system. The other part is the peripheral nervous system, which consists of nerves that connect the brain and spinal cord to the rest of the body. The central nervous system is the body's processing center.
Schwann cells (SCs) are the main glial cells of the peripheral nervous system which wrap around axons of motor and sensory neurons to form the myelin sheath.

The limbic system is the part of the brain involved in our behavioral and emotional responses, especially when it comes to behaviors we need for survival: feeding, reproduction and caring for our young, and fight or flight responses.

A sympathetic nervous system is a network of nerves that helps your body activate its “fight-or-flight” response. This system's activity increases when you're stressed, in danger or physically active.

questions.explanation: Schwann cells and satellite glial are the two main glial cell types of the peripheral nervous system (PNS). While satellite glia are found within ganglia in close association with neuronal somata, schwann cells are found in close contact with axons in the peripheral nerves.

questions.text: Type of neurons found exclusively in CNS are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons are responsible for transmitting sensory information from the peripheral nervous system to the central nervous system; they are not exclusive to the CNS.
Motor neurons carry signals from the central nervous system to muscles and glands, but they are not found exclusively in the CNS.
Associative neurons, also known as interneurons, are found exclusively within the central nervous system, connecting sensory and motor pathways.
This option is incorrect as associative neurons are indeed found exclusively in the CNS.

questions.explanation: Associative neurons, also referred to as interneurons, are found solely within the central nervous system (CNS), which includes the brain and spinal cord. They play a critical role in processing information and relaying signals between sensory and motor neurons. Unlike sensory neurons, which are activated by external stimuli and are part of the peripheral nervous system, and motor neurons, which transmit commands from the CNS to the body, associative neurons are confined to the CNS. Therefore, the correct answer is associative neurons, as they are the only type of neuron found exclusively in the CNS.

questions.text: Which of the following are found in the nose to detect smell?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Olfactory receptors are specialized sensory cells located in the nasal cavity. They detect odor molecules, allowing us to perceive different smells. These receptors are part of the olfactory system, which is directly associated with the sense of smell.
Photoreceptors are cells found in the retina of the eye, responsible for detecting light and enabling vision. They are not related to the sense of smell.
Phonoreceptors are sensory receptors involved in the detection of sound. They are found in the ear, not the nose, and are involved in the auditory system, not the olfactory system.
Gustatory receptors are involved in the sense of taste, located on the tongue and other areas of the mouth. They are not involved in the detection of smell.

questions.explanation: The olfactory epithelium contains special receptors (called olfactory receptors) that are sensitive to odor molecules that travel through the air. These receptors are very small.

questions.text: Why is there no glucose present in the distal of a nephron?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Glucose molecules are too large to pass across the basement membrane.
options.explanation: This is incorrect because glucose molecules are small enough to be filtered through the glomerulus and are not prevented by size from entering the nephron.

options.text: Glucose is removed by osmosis from the tubule.
options.explanation: This is incorrect as osmosis involves the passive movement of water, not glucose. Glucose reabsorption involves active transport mechanisms.

options.text: Glucose is passively absorbed by the cells lining the descending loop of Henle.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The descending loop of Henle does not absorb glucose. It is involved in the reabsorption of water, while glucose reabsorption occurs in the proximal tubule.

options.text: Glucose is reabsorbed by the proximal tubule cells.
options.explanation: Correct. Glucose is actively reabsorbed by transport proteins in the proximal tubule, ensuring it does not reach the distal parts of the nephron.

questions.explanation: Glucose is initially filtered through the glomerulus into the nephron, but it is actively reabsorbed by transport proteins in the proximal tubule cells. This mechanism ensures that glucose does not reach the distal parts of the nephron, making it absent there. Option A is incorrect because glucose is small enough to be filtered. Option B is incorrect because osmosis does not apply to glucose reabsorption. Option C is incorrect because glucose reabsorption does not occur in the descending loop of Henle.

questions.text: The actual visual receptor of the eye are :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Iris and Pupil: The iris is the colored part of the eye that controls the size of the pupil, which is the opening in the center of the iris. However, these structures are not the actual visual receptors responsible for detecting light and forming images.

b) Iris and Lens: The iris regulates the amount of light entering the eye by adjusting the size of the pupil, and the lens helps focus light onto the retina. While the lens is involved in focusing light, it is not the actual visual receptor.

c) Rods and Cones: The correct answer. Rods and cones are specialized cells in the retina of the eye that are responsible for detecting light and color. Rods are more sensitive to low levels of light and are important for night vision, while cones are responsible for color vision and visual acuity in brighter conditions.

d) Cornea and Retina: The cornea is the clear front surface of the eye that helps focus light onto the retina. The retina, located at the back of the eye, contains the actual visual receptors (rods and cones) that detect light and transmit visual signals to the brain.

questions.explanation: Cones are active at higher light levels (photopic vision), are capable of colour vision and are responsible for high spatial acuity. Iris is the coloured part, pupil is the central black spot from where light enters the eye. Lens is responsible for focusing light. Cornea is a membrane that protects structures inside eye while retina contains layers of photoreceptors (rods and cones).

questions.text: The motor nerve cell transmits impulses from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The effector organ to spinal cord.
options.explanation: This is not possible.
This is the function of sensory neurons.
This is not the function of motor nerve cells.
Motor neurons transport CNS impulses to effector organs. Efferent neurons are another name for them. Muscles or glands that act in response to motor neuron stimulation are known as effectors.

questions.explanation: (Right answer): The correct sequence of transmission of impulse is Sensory Neuron→Spinal cord→Motor neuron→Effector.Motor neurons transport CNS impulses to effector organs. Efferent neurons are another name for them. Muscles or glands that act in response to motor neuron stimulation are known as effectors.

questions.text: Sensors that receive stimulus:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Receptors are specialized structures that are directly responsible for detecting environmental stimuli and converting them into signals that can be understood by the nervous system. They function as the initial point of contact with the external or internal environment.

While neurons play a critical role in transmitting and processing the signals generated by receptors, they do not directly receive environmental stimuli themselves. Instead, they act as intermediaries, carrying information from receptors to other parts of the nervous system.

Effectors are the structures, such as muscles or glands, that execute responses based on the signals they receive from neurons. They do not detect stimuli but rather react to the processed signals sent by the nervous system.

This option is incorrect because, although all components are part of the sensory-motor pathway, only receptors are directly responsible for detecting and responding to stimuli. Neurons and effectors serve subsequent roles in signal transmission and response execution, respectively.

questions.explanation: Receptors are specialized structures that detect and respond to specific stimuli in the environment. They transmit this information to neurons for further processing.

questions.text: Cell bodies of _ neurons are always located inside the CNS.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons carry information from sensory receptors to the CNS. Their cell bodies are usually found outside the CNS in structures called ganglia.
Motor neurons are responsible for transmitting signals from the CNS to muscles and glands. Their cell bodies are generally located within the CNS, but not exclusively so.
Associative neurons, or interneurons, are entirely confined to the CNS. They serve as connectors between sensory and motor neurons, facilitating communication within the CNS.
Reflex arcs involve sensory, motor, and interneurons. While the reflex action is rapid and involves the CNS, not all neurons involved have cell bodies located within the CNS.

questions.explanation: Interneurons, also known as associative neurons, are a type of neuron that primarily functions to relay signals between other neurons. They are located entirely within the central nervous system (CNS), connecting sensory neurons to motor neurons and facilitating communication and processing of information within the CNS.

questions.text: Which part of the central nervous system controls the body’s water balance?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cerebellum primarily coordinates voluntary movements and balance, not water balance.
The cerebrum is responsible for higher brain functions like thought and action, not directly involved in water balance.
The hypothalamus regulates water balance by controlling the release of antidiuretic hormone (ADH), which influences kidney function to maintain water homeostasis.
The medulla controls autonomic functions such as breathing and heart rate, not water balance.

questions.explanation: Hypothalamus is responsible for producing the water balance as it controls the release of a hormone called antidiuretic hormone(ADH), which acts on the collecting duct cells.

questions.text: Nociceptors are specialized for the detection of sense of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Specialized peripheral sensory neurons known as nociceptors alert us to potentially damaging stimuli at the skin by detecting extremes in temperature and pressure and injury-related chemicals, and transducing these stimuli into long-ranging electrical signals that are relayed to higher brain centers.

Specialized peripheral sensory neurons known as nociceptors alert us to potentially damaging stimuli at the skin by detecting extremes in temperature and pressure and injury-related chemicals, and transducing these stimuli into long-ranging electrical signals that are relayed to higher brain centers.

Specialized peripheral sensory neurons known as nociceptors alert us to potentially damaging stimuli at the skin by detecting extremes in temperature and pressure and injury-related chemicals, and transducing these stimuli into long-ranging electrical signals that are relayed to higher brain centers.

Nociceptors are equipped with specific molecular sensors, which detect extreme heat or cold and certain harmful chemicals. Specialized peripheral sensory neurons known as nociceptors alert us to potentially damaging stimuli at the skin by detecting extremes in temperature and pressure and injury-related chemicals, and transducing these stimuli into long-ranging electrical signals that are relayed to higher brain centers.

questions.explanation: Specialized peripheral sensory neurons known as nociceptors alert us to potentially damaging stimuli at the skin by detecting extremes in temperature and pressure and injury-related chemicals, and transducing these stimuli into long-ranging electrical signals that are relayed to higher brain centers.

questions.text: Speech and language areas are located in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The thalamus is involved in relaying sensory information to the cerebral cortex, but it does not specifically handle language or speech processing.
The medulla oblongata regulates autonomic functions such as breathing and heart rate, and is not involved in language or speech.
The right hemisphere is more involved in spatial and non-verbal tasks, rather than language and speech, which are primarily managed by the left hemisphere.
The left cerebral hemisphere is predominantly responsible for language and speech, containing critical areas like Broca's and Wernicke's, which facilitate speech production and comprehension.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is the left cerebral hemisphere. This hemisphere is crucial for language and speech functions, containing key areas such as Broca's and Wernicke's areas. These regions are essential for the production and understanding of speech. In contrast, the thalamus serves as a relay station for sensory information, the medulla oblongata manages autonomic functions, and the right cerebral hemisphere is more involved in spatial and non-verbal tasks, not language processing.

questions.text: The sense of hearing is concerned with:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cerebrum is responsible for processing sensory information, including sight, smell, taste, speech, and hearing. It plays a key role in interpreting sound.
The cerebellum primarily coordinates voluntary movements and is involved in motor control. It is not directly involved in processing sensory information like hearing.
The medulla regulates autonomic functions such as heartbeat and breathing, but it is not involved in the sense of hearing.
The hypothalamus is involved in maintaining homeostasis and regulating bodily functions, but it does not process sensory information like hearing.

questions.explanation: The cerebrum is the largest part of the brain and is responsible for processing and interpreting sensory information, including sound, which is why it is associated with the sense of hearing. The other options, such as the cerebellum, medulla, and hypothalamus, have different functions related to movement coordination, autonomic regulation, and homeostasis, respectively, and do not directly handle auditory processing.

questions.text: Receptor sensitive to smell are:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Mechanoreceptors are sensitive to stimuli such as touch,vibration,pressure and sound.
Chemical receptors are sensitive to chemicals and they help differentiate and recognize taste,smell and detect changes in O,2 ,CO2 and Ph.
Photoreceptors are sensitive to light and convert light energy to electrical signals which are sent to the brain. They also give us night vision and color.

questions.explanation: Chemical receptors are sensitive to chemicals and they help differentiate and recognize taste,smell and detect changes in O2 ,CO2 and Ph.

questions.text: Baroreceptors are found in _
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Baroreceptors are primarily found in the walls of blood vessels. They are specialized sensory receptors that detect changes in blood pressure. When blood pressure increases, baroreceptors send signals to the brain to initiate mechanisms that help regulate and maintain blood pressure within a normal range.

Incorrect.Baroreceptors are primarily found in the walls of blood vessels. They are specialized sensory receptors that detect changes in blood pressure. When blood pressure increases, baroreceptors send signals to the brain to initiate mechanisms that help regulate and maintain blood pressure within a normal range.

Incorrect.Baroreceptors are primarily found in the walls of blood vessels. They are specialized sensory receptors that detect changes in blood pressure. When blood pressure increases, baroreceptors send signals to the brain to initiate mechanisms that help regulate and maintain blood pressure within a normal range.

Incorrect.Baroreceptors are primarily found in the walls of blood vessels. They are specialized sensory receptors that detect changes in blood pressure. When blood pressure increases, baroreceptors send signals to the brain to initiate mechanisms that help regulate and maintain blood pressure within a normal range.

questions.explanation: Baroreceptors are primarily found in the walls of blood vessels. They are specialized sensory receptors that detect changes in blood pressure. When blood pressure increases, baroreceptors send signals to the brain to initiate mechanisms that help regulate and maintain blood pressure within a normal range.

questions.text: Nerve fibre is different from the muscle fibre due to the presence of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Myofibrils are thread-like structures found within muscle fibers. They are responsible for muscle contraction and are not present in nerve fibers.
"Lines" is a vague term in this context and doesn't refer to a specific structure in either nerve fibers or muscle fibers.
The sarcolemma is the cell membrane of a muscle fiber. It is crucial for muscle contraction but is not a feature that distinguishes nerve fibers from muscle fibers.

Dendrites are specialized extensions of nerve cells that receive electrical signals from other nerve cells and transmit them towards the cell body. They are a distinctive feature of nerve cells and are not present in muscle fibers.

questions.explanation: Dendrites are specialized extensions of nerve cells that receive electrical signals from other nerve cells and transmit them towards the cell body. They are a distinctive feature of nerve cells and are not present in muscle fibers. Therefore, option d is correct.

questions.text: Telencephalon refers to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The brain stem is a different part of the brain, located below the telencephalon. It is responsible for basic life functions like breathing, heart rate, and consciousness.
The cerebellum is located at the back of the brain, underneath the cerebral hemispheres. It is primarily involved in coordinating motor movements and balance, rather than higher cognitive functions.

While the cortex is part of the telencephalon, it specifically refers to the outer layer of the cerebral hemispheres. It plays a crucial role in various cognitive functions like perception, attention, and language processing, but it is just one component of the telencephalon.

The telencephalon is the most anterior part of the brain and is responsible for higher cognitive functions such as perception, learning, and memory. It includes the cerebral hemispheres, which make up the largest portion of the human brain.

questions.explanation: The telencephalon is the most anterior part of the brain and is responsible for higher cognitive functions such as perception, learning, and memory. It includes the cerebral hemispheres, which make up the largest portion of the human brain. Therefore, option d is correct.

questions.text: Tactition receptors are abundant in human:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tactition receptors are abundant in the human sole, which is the bottom part of the foot. These receptors are responsible for detecting and relaying information about touch and pressure sensations. They help us perceive textures, balance, and navigate our surroundings. So, the sole of the foot is an important area for tactile sensitivity.

Tactition receptors are abundant in the human sole, which is the bottom part of the foot. These receptors are responsible for detecting and relaying information about touch and pressure sensations. They help us perceive textures, balance, and navigate our surroundings. So, the sole of the foot is an important area for tactile sensitivity.

Tactition receptors are abundant in the human sole, which is the bottom part of the foot. These receptors are responsible for detecting and relaying information about touch and pressure sensations. They help us perceive textures, balance, and navigate our surroundings. So, the sole of the foot is an important area for tactile sensitivity.

Tactition receptors are abundant in the human sole, which is the bottom part of the foot. These receptors are responsible for detecting and relaying information about touch and pressure sensations. They help us perceive textures, balance, and navigate our surroundings. So, the sole of the foot is an important area for tactile sensitivity.

questions.explanation: Tactition receptors are abundant in the human sole, which is the bottom part of the foot. These receptors are responsible for detecting and relaying information about touch and pressure sensations. They help us perceive textures, balance, and navigate our surroundings. So, the sole of the foot is an important area for tactile sensitivity.

questions.text: Cerebellum is involved in
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These reflex actions are primarily mediated by the spinal cord, not the cerebellum.
While nerve impulses involve multiple parts of the nervous system, the cerebellum specifically focuses on the coordination of muscle movements.
Homeostasis involves processes regulated by other brain regions such as the hypothalamus, not the cerebellum.
The cerebellum is crucial for the fine-tuning and coordination of voluntary muscle movements, making this the correct choice.

questions.explanation: The cerebellum plays a key role in the coordination and fine control of muscle movements, making it the most relevant option.

questions.text: The spinal card serves as the center of
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the spinal cord plays a role in transmitting sensory information and coordinating reflex actions, it is not the center of subconscious thought. Subconscious thought involves higher brain functions.

Reflex actions are rapid, involuntary responses to a specific stimulus that occur without conscious thought. The spinal cord is responsible for coordinating many of these reflex actions, allowing the body to respond quickly to various sensory inputs.
Habits are primarily associated with the brain's regions responsible for memory and learning, not the spinal cord.
Tropisms are the turning or bending of an organism in response to a directional stimulus, such as the growth of a plant toward light. This is not directly related to the functions of the spinal cord.

Synapses are the junctions where nerve cells communicate, and they occur throughout the nervous system, including the spinal cord. However, the spinal cord is not specifically the center of synapses but a part of the nervous system where these connections occur.

questions.explanation: Reflex actions are rapid, involuntary responses to a specific stimulus that occur without conscious thought. The spinal cord is responsible for coordinating many of these reflex actions, allowing the body to respond quickly to various sensory inputs.

questions.text: The breathing rate is controlled by the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While the lungs are responsible for the physical act of breathing (inhalation and exhalation), they do not control the breathing rate. The rate and depth of breathing are regulated by the brain, particularly the medulla.

The medulla oblongata, a region in the brainstem, plays a crucial role in regulating basic life-sustaining functions, including the breathing rate. It receives signals from chemoreceptors that monitor blood gas levels (primarily carbon dioxide) and adjusts the breathing rate to maintain proper oxygen and carbon dioxide levels in the blood.
The cerebrum is responsible for higher-order brain functions, such as conscious thought, perception, and voluntary actions, but it is not the primary controller of the breathing rate.
The cerebellum is mainly involved in coordinating motor movements and maintaining balance, but it is not the primary regulator of the breathing rate.

Sensory areas in the brain process sensory information, but they do not directly control the breathing rate. The regulation of breathing rate occurs in the brainstem, particularly in the medulla oblongata.

questions.explanation: The medulla oblongata, a region in the brainstem, plays a crucial role in regulating basic life-sustaining functions, including the breathing rate. It receives signals from chemoreceptors that monitor blood gas levels (primarily carbon dioxide) and adjusts the breathing rate to maintain proper oxygen and carbon dioxide levels in the blood.

questions.text: The sense of balance. is maintained with the help of the
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The cochlea is another part of the inner ear responsible for hearing, not the sense of balance.
The hammer, also known as the malleus, is one of the three small bones in the middle ear that transmit sound vibrations to the inner ear. It is involved in hearing but not balance.
The anvil, also known as the incus, is another of the three small bones in the middle ear that transmits sound vibrations to the inner ear. It is involved in hearing but not balance.

The stirrup, also known as the stapes, is the third of the three small bones in the middle ear responsible for transmitting sound vibrations to the inner ear. Like the hammer and anvil, it is involved in hearing and not the sense of balance.
The semicircular canals are part of the inner ear and play a crucial role in detecting changes in head position and motion, which are essential for maintaining balance and spatial orientation.

questions.explanation: The semicircular canals are part of the inner ear and play a crucial role in detecting changes in head position and motion, which are essential for maintaining balance and spatial orientation.

questions.text: Nissl granules are the collection of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is partially correct because Nissl granules are associated with rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER), but ribosomes are also a key component. So, this option alone is incomplete.
Incorrect. Golgi bodies are involved in protein modification and packaging but are not part of Nissl granules.
Correct. Nissl granules consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) and ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis in neurons.
Incorrect. Mitochondria are involved in energy production (ATP synthesis) but do not form Nissl granules.

questions.explanation: Correct. Nissl granules consist of rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) and ribosomes, which are essential for protein synthesis in neurons.

questions.text: The region where the impulse moves from one neuron to other neuron is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Receive signals but don't transmit them to another neuron directly.

A synapse is the junction between two neurons or between a neuron and another cell (like muscle). It consists of a presynaptic terminal, synaptic cleft, and postsynaptic membrane. When an electrical impulse reaches the synapse, it triggers the release of neurotransmitters which carry the signal across the gap.
It is the electrical impulse itself, not a structure.
Carry impulses away from the cell body but don't represent the junction.

questions.explanation: A synapse is the junction between two neurons or between a neuron and another cell (like muscle). It consists of a presynaptic terminal, synaptic cleft, and postsynaptic membrane. When an electrical impulse reaches the synapse, it triggers the release of neurotransmitters which carry the signal across the gap.

questions.text: The diagram shows a motor nerve cell. Which part of the cell contains a transmitter substance:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Likely refers to dendrites, which receive signals, not transmit them.
Could be the cell body, which processes inputs, but doesn't store neurotransmitters.

The axon terminal (C) contains synaptic vesicles that store neurotransmitters such as acetylcholine. These chemicals are released into the synaptic cleft during nerve impulses to transmit signals to the next neuron or muscle cell.This is critical for communication in the neuromuscular junction and other synapses.
Often represents the axon, which conducts impulses but doesn't store transmitters.

questions.explanation: The axon terminal (C) contains synaptic vesicles that store neurotransmitters such as acetylcholine. These chemicals are released into the synaptic cleft during nerve impulses to transmit signals to the next neuron or muscle cell.This is critical for communication in the neuromuscular junction and other synapses.

questions.text: Which of the following receptor types detects changes in the total solute concentration of the blood and stimulates thirst when osmolarity increases?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These detect mechanical forces like pressure, stretch, or vibration, not solute concentration.

Osmoreceptors are located primarily in the hypothalamus and detect changes in theosmolarity (solute concentration) of body fluids. When blood becomes too concentrated (high osmolarity), they stimulate thirst and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) release to conserve water. ADH enhances water reabsorption in kidneys, helping to restore normal osmotic balance.
These respond to changes in temperature, not osmolarity.
These detect light in the retina and have no role in fluid regulation.

questions.explanation: Osmoreceptors are located primarily in the hypothalamus and detect changes in theosmolarity (solute concentration) of body fluids. When blood becomes too concentrated (high osmolarity), they stimulate thirst and antidiuretic hormone (ADH) release to conserve water. ADH enhances water reabsorption in kidneys, helping to restore normal osmotic balance.

questions.text: “Nociceptors” are sensory receptors that respond to which type of stimulus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: These are detected by mechanoreceptors, not nociceptors.

Nociceptors are pain receptors that respond to potentially damaging stimuli, such as extreme heat, pressure, or chemicals released from injured tissues. They play a key role in protective reflexes and awareness of injury. They transmit pain signals to the CNS, initiating protective mechanisms like withdrawal reflexes and promoting awareness to prevent further harm.
Detected by osmoreceptors, not related to pain perception.
Detected by photoreceptors in the eye, not involved in pain sensing.

questions.explanation: Nociceptors are pain receptors that respond to potentially damaging stimuli, such as extreme heat, pressure, or chemicals released from injured tissues. They play a key role in protective reflexes and awareness of injury. They transmit pain signals to the CNS, initiating protective mechanisms like withdrawal reflexes and promoting awareness to prevent further harm.

questions.text: In the neuron diagram, which organelle is indicated as the site of extensive protein synthesis for neurotransmitter production?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the region where the action potential initiates, not involved in protein synthesis.

Nissl bodies, also known as rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) in neurons, are heavily involved in protein synthesis, particularly for the production of enzymes and proteins, including those used in neurotransmitter synthesis. These structures are concentrated near the nucleus and are visible as the "rough endoplasmic reticulum" in the labeled diagram. Their ribosomes give them a granular appearance.
A lipid-rich insulating layer formed by glial cells; not involved in protein production.
Produces the myelin sheath in the PNS; it is a supporting cell, not part of the neuron's protein machinery

questions.explanation: Nissl bodies, also known as rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) in neurons, are heavily involved in protein synthesis, particularly for the production of enzymes and proteins, including those used in neurotransmitter synthesis. These structures are concentrated near the nucleus and are visible as the "rough endoplasmic reticulum" in the labeled diagram. Their ribosomes give them a granular appearance.

questions.text: Which neuron type acts as a mediator between sensory and motor neurons, facilitating information processing within the central nervous system?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons bring information into the CNS but don’t integrate it.
Motor neurons carry signals out, not process them.

Interneurons, also known as association neurons, are found entirely within the CNS (brain and spinal cord). They receive input from sensory neurons and relay signals to motor neurons. They play a critical role in information processing, reflexes, and decision-making. Most neurons in the CNS are interneurons.
Schwann cells are supporting cells that form myelin in the PNS, not neurons.

questions.explanation: Interneurons, also known as association neurons, are found entirely within the CNS (brain and spinal cord). They receive input from sensory neurons and relay signals to motor neurons. They play a critical role in information processing, reflexes, and decision-making. Most neurons in the CNS are interneurons.

questions.text: Which neuron carries impulses away from the central nervous system to muscles or glands?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Sensory neurons carry information into the CNS.
Interneurons only relay and integrate signals within the CNS.

Motor (efferent) neurons carry signals from the CNS to effectors like muscles or glands to initiate a response. "Efferent = Exit the CNS" "Afferent = Arrive at the CNS”Motor (efferent) neurons transmit impulses from the CNS to effectors such as muscles or glands, initiating a response. This is the final step in the reflex arc or motor pathway. The word “efferent” helps you remember that the signal exits the CNS.
Photoreceptors are sensory cells, not neurons, and are specialized for detecting light.

questions.explanation: Motor (efferent) neurons carry signals from the CNS to effectors like muscles or glands to initiate a response. "Efferent = Exit the CNS" "Afferent = Arrive at the CNS”Motor (efferent) neurons transmit impulses from the CNS to effectors such as muscles or glands, initiating a response. This is the final step in the reflex arc or motor pathway. The word “efferent” helps you remember that the signal exits the CNS.

questions.text: Which type of receptor in the skin is responsible for detecting deep pressure?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Detect light touch and are located in the superficial dermis.
Detect skin stretch and sustained pressure but not deep pressure.

Pacinian corpuscles, also known as lamellar corpuscles, are large, onion-shaped mechanoreceptors found deep in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. They are highly sensitive to deep pressure and high-frequency vibration, allowing the body to detect and respond to mechanical changes from deeper sources.
Detect steady pressure and texture; found closer to the skin surface.

questions.explanation: Pacinian corpuscles, also known as lamellar corpuscles, are large, onion-shaped mechanoreceptors found deep in the dermis and subcutaneous tissue. They are highly sensitive to deep pressure and high-frequency vibration, allowing the body to detect and respond to mechanical changes from deeper sources.

questions.text: How many basic taste perceptions do humans recognize?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Outdated classifications; they do not include umami.
Outdated classifications; they do not include umami.

The five basic tastes are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami.Humans recognize five basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami. Umami, the most recently identified, is the taste of glutamate and is commonly found in protein-rich foods. Each taste is detected by specific receptors located on taste buds.
No sixth basic taste has been widely accepted in standard classification.

questions.explanation: The five basic tastes are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami.Humans recognize five basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami. Umami, the most recently identified, is the taste of glutamate and is commonly found in protein-rich foods. Each taste is detected by specific receptors located on taste buds.

questions.text: Which receptor type is incorrectly matched with its function?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Ruffini's corpuscles — Detect skin stretch and sustained pressure and temperature
options.explanation: This option is correctly matched. Ruffini's corpuscles are mechanoreceptors located deep in the skin that respond to sustained pressure, skin stretch, and temperature changes, contributing significantly to our sense of proprioception and awareness of body position.

options.text: Meissner's corpuscles — Respond to light touch in hairy skin
options.explanation: This option is incorrectly matched. Meissner's corpuscles are specialized mechanoreceptors that primarily respond to light touch and are predominantly found in glabrous (hairless) skin, such as the fingertips and palms, not in hairy skin.

options.text: Pacinian corpuscles — Sense deep pressure and vibration
options.explanation: This option is correctly matched. Pacinian corpuscles are located deep within the dermis and subcutaneous tissues, where they are highly sensitive to deep pressure and high-frequency vibrations, making them essential for detecting changes in pressure and texture.

options.text: Merkel's disks — Monitor static touch and texture
options.explanation: This option is correctly matched. Merkel's disks are found in the basal layer of the epidermis and are responsible for detecting static touch and texture, which aids in our ability to perceive fine details and shapes.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Option B: Meissner's corpuscles — Respond to light touch in hairy skin. This option is incorrectly matched because Meissner's corpuscles are primarily located in glabrous (hairless) skin, such as the fingertips and palms, where they are specialized for detecting light touch and low-frequency vibrations. The other options are correctly matched: Option A accurately describes the function of Ruffini's corpuscles, Option C accurately describes Pacinian corpuscles' sensitivity to deep pressure and vibration, and Option D correctly explains the role of Merkel's disks in monitoring static touch and texture. Understanding the specific locations and functions of these mechanoreceptors is essential for accurately matching them.

questions.text: Refer to Fig.17.22 (Receptors in Human Skin).Which labeled structure is responsible for detecting rapid changes in touch, such as light tapping, and is found in hairless areas like fingertips?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Detect sustained pressure and skin stretch; adapt slowly.
Detect deep pressure and vibration, not light touch.
Detect steady pressure and texture, not rapid changes.

Meissner's corpuscles are rapidly adapting mechanoreceptors located in the dermal papillae of glabrous (hairless) skin, especially in areas like the fingertips, palms, and soles. They are highly sensitive to light touch and dynamic changes, such as fluttering or tapping stimuli, making them crucial for detecting fine textures and movements.

questions.explanation: Meissner's corpuscles are rapidly adapting mechanoreceptors located in the dermal papillae of glabrous (hairless) skin, especially in areas like the fingertips, palms, and soles. They are highly sensitive to light touch and dynamic changes, such as fluttering or tapping stimuli, making them crucial for detecting fine textures and movements.

questions.text: Which structure on the tongue houses the taste buds and appears as nipple-like projections in the image? (taste receptors)
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Related to smell, located in the nasal cavity.

Papillae contain taste buds and give the tongue its textured appearance.Papillae are small, nipple-shaped projections on the tongue that house taste buds. There are several types—fungiform, foliate, circumvallate—each containing taste receptors that detect the five primary tastes. They also contribute to the rough texture of the tongue.
Contain sensory neuron cell bodies; unrelated to taste.
Detect pain, not taste.

questions.explanation: Papillae contain taste buds and give the tongue its textured appearance.Papillae are small, nipple-shaped projections on the tongue that house taste buds. There are several types—fungiform, foliate, circumvallate—each containing taste receptors that detect the five primary tastes. They also contribute to the rough texture of the tongue.

questions.text: Nociceptor is a type of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Chemoreceptors detect chemical stimuli, such as taste and smell, but they are not involved in the sensation of pain.
Thermoreceptors are sensitive to changes in temperature, detecting heat and cold, but they do not specifically detect pain.
Photoreceptors are specialized cells found in the retina of the eye that detect light, not pain.
Nociceptors are specialized sensory receptors that detect pain and potentially damaging stimuli, serving as the body's alert system for harm.

questions.explanation: Nociceptors are a type of sensory receptor specifically responsible for detecting signals associated with pain and potential tissue damage. They play a crucial role in alerting the body to harmful stimuli, prompting protective responses. In contrast, chemoreceptors, thermoreceptors, and photoreceptors are specialized for detecting chemical stimuli, temperature changes, and light, respectively, and are not involved in pain detection.

questions.text: The value showing the voltage going from a resting neuron to a depolarized neuron is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This range indicates a hyperpolarization or a more negative state, not depolarization.
This range doesn't represent a typical depolarization phase as it starts with a positive value.
This is the correct range for depolarization, where the neuron's membrane potential moves from resting (-70 mV) to a more positive state (0 mV).
This range represents repolarization, where the potential returns to resting state, not depolarization.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is –70 mV to +0 mV. During depolarization, a neuron's membrane potential shifts from its resting state of approximately –70 mV towards 0 mV as sodium ions enter the cell. This change makes the inside of the neuron less negative compared to the outside. The other options do not accurately describe this process: Option A describes hyperpolarization, Option B has a range that does not typically occur, and Option D describes repolarization.

questions.text: Olfactory receptors lie in:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Taste buds are involved in the gustatory system, which is different from the olfactory system.
Rods and cones are photoreceptors found in the retina of the eyes, responsible for vision, not smell.
Nasal hairs are responsible for filtering particles from the air, but they are not involved in detecting smells.
The nasal epithelium contains olfactory receptors, which are specialized for detecting odors.

questions.explanation: Olfactory receptors are located in the nasal epithelium. These receptors are responsible for detecting odor molecules and sending signals to the brain to perceive smell. The other options, such as taste buds, rods and cones, and nasal hairs, are part of different sensory systems or functions. Taste buds are part of the gustatory system, rods and cones are part of the visual system, and nasal hairs serve a protective function rather than sensory detection.

questions.text: The basic unit of nervous system is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) is a clear fluid found in the brain and spine but is not a cell.
The central nervous system (CNS) and peripheral nervous system (PNS) are major components of the nervous system, but they are not individual cells.
Schwann cells are specific to the peripheral nervous system and aid in nerve regeneration and myelination, but they are not the basic unit.
Neurons are the fundamental units of the brain and nervous system, responsible for receiving sensory input and sending motor commands.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Neuron. Neurons are the basic working units of the nervous system, specialized to transmit information throughout the body. They are the primary cells responsible for receiving and transmitting signals. CSF, while important, is a fluid, not a cell. CNS and PNS refer to systems, not individual cells. Schwann cells support neurons but are not the basic unit.

questions.text: The _ is triggered when any stimulus is received by the neuron:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The resting membrane potential is the electrical potential across the neuron's membrane when it is not being stimulated. It is not triggered by a stimulus.
An action potential is the sudden change in electrical potential that occurs when a neuron is stimulated and reaches the threshold. It is the correct response to a stimulus.
The threshold potential is the level of membrane depolarization that must be reached in order to trigger an action potential. It is not itself the response triggered by a stimulus.
A sub-threshold potential is a change in membrane potential that is not strong enough to trigger an action potential. It is not the correct response to a stimulus.

questions.explanation: An action potential is triggered when a stimulus causes the neuron's membrane potential to reach a certain threshold. This results in a rapid depolarization and repolarization of the neuron, allowing the signal to be transmitted along the nerve. The resting membrane potential is the state of the neuron when it is not being stimulated. The threshold potential is the critical level that must be reached to trigger an action potential, but it is not the response itself. Sub-threshold potentials are changes in membrane potential that are insufficient to trigger an action potential.

questions.text: Olfactory receptors are the type of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Photoreceptors are specialized cells that respond to light and are primarily found in the retina of the eye, enabling vision. They are not involved in the sense of smell.
Mechanoreceptors detect mechanical changes such as pressure, vibration, and touch. They play a role in the sense of touch and hearing, not in olfaction.
Thermoreceptors are responsible for sensing temperature changes. They do not play a role in detecting chemical stimuli, which is essential for the sense of smell.
Chemoreceptors are specialized receptors that respond to chemical stimuli. Olfactory receptors are a type of chemoreceptor that detects airborne chemicals, enabling the sense of smell.

questions.explanation: Olfactory receptors are classified as chemoreceptors because they detect chemical substances in the air. When these chemicals bind to the olfactory receptors in the nasal cavity, they initiate a signal that is interpreted by the brain as smell. The other options, such as photoreceptors, mechanoreceptors, and thermoreceptors, are specialized for different sensory modalities—vision, touch, and temperature, respectively—and do not relate to the olfactory system.

questions.text: Which of the following cells possess Nissl's granules?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Nerve cells, or neurons, are characterized by the presence of Nissl's granules, which are composed of rough endoplasmic reticulum and ribosomes, essential for protein synthesis.
White blood cells (WBCs) do not have Nissl's granules; they are part of the immune system and lack the specialized structures found in neurons.
Red blood cells (RBCs) are primarily involved in gas transport and do not contain Nissl's granules or any organelles, as they are enucleated.
Platelets are cell fragments involved in clotting and do not possess Nissl's granules, as they are not derived from nerve cells.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is nerve cells, as they contain Nissl's granules, which are indicative of their active role in protein synthesis necessary for neurotransmitter production and overall cellular function. In contrast, white blood cells (WBCs), red blood cells (RBCs), and platelets do not contain these granules. WBCs are part of the immune system, RBCs are mainly for oxygen transport and lack organelles entirely, and platelets are involved in blood clotting but are not true cells, lacking nuclei and organelles found in nerve cells.

questions.text: Which type of neurons stimulate muscles to contract in a reflex arc?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Motor neurons are responsible for transmitting signals from the central nervous system to muscles, leading to muscle contraction in reflex actions.
Sensory neurons carry signals from sensory receptors to the central nervous system but do not directly stimulate muscle contractions.
Interneurons connect sensory and motor neurons within the central nervous system but do not directly stimulate muscles to contract.
Relay neurons is another term often used interchangeably with interneurons, which help in processing information but do not directly cause muscle contraction.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is Motor neurons because they are specifically responsible for carrying impulses from the central nervous system to skeletal muscles, leading to muscle contraction in response to a reflex action. In a reflex arc, a stimulus activates sensory neurons, which send signals to interneurons in the spinal cord. The interneurons then relay the signal to motor neurons, which ultimately stimulate the muscles to contract. Sensory neurons (Option B) are involved in transmitting sensory information but do not cause muscle contraction. Interneurons (Option C) serve as connectors within the central nervous system and do not directly affect muscle activity. Relay neurons (Option D) are often synonymous with interneurons and also do not stimulate muscle contractions directly.
